risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Ninety patients were randomized 2:1 to the treatment arm using a minimization algorithm in an effort to balance the groups for disease severity (29). Groups were stratified according to baseline six minute walk test with a threshold of 300 meters and baseline upright forced vital capacity with a threshold of 55% of the predicted value." Comment: The study explicitly describes use of a minimization algorithm with stratification variables to allocate participants, which is an acceptable random allocation method under Cochrane guidance and indicates a systematic random sequence generation process rather than a predictable non-random rule.

Quote: "Ninety patients were randomized 2:1 to the treatment arm using a minimization algorithm in an effort to balance the groups for disease severity (29). Groups were stratified according to baseline six minute walk test with a threshold of 300 meters and baseline upright forced vital capacity with a threshold of 55% of the predicted value." Comment: This describes the randomization approach/sequence structure (minimization and stratification) but does not explicitly report any allocation concealment mechanism such as central randomization, pharmacy control, or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes. Therefore, whether the person enrolling participants could foresee the next assignment is not stated.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This study was a post-hoc analysis of cardiovascular parameters in a double-blind, multi-center, randomized controlled trial evaluating the efficacy and safety of acid alpha-glucosidase enzyme replacement therapy versus placebo..." and "The treatment arm received bi-weekly infusions of enzyme replacement therapy at a dose of 20 mg/kg for the 78 week study period, and the control group received a volume matched placebo infusion." Comment: The trial is explicitly described as double-blind and used a volume-matched placebo infusion, supporting blinding of participants and treating personnel. The dominant outcomes in this study context are largely objective/standardized (e.g. FVC, ECG/echocardiographic measures), so even if blinding were imperfect, these outcomes are less likely to be materially influenced by knowledge of allocation.

Quote: "This study was a post-hoc analysis of cardiovascular parameters in a double-blind, multi-center, randomized controlled trial..." and "All ECG’s were interpreted at the central cardiology core lab by a cardiologist blinded to treatment arm." and "All echocardiograms were interpreted at the central cardiology core lab by a cardiologist blinded to treatment allocation." Comment: The outcome assessors were independent clinician assessors/core-lab cardiologists blinded to allocation. The outcomes assessed in this report (ECG and echocardiographic measures such as PR interval, QRS duration, QTc, ejection fraction, LV mass index) are instrument-based cardiovascular measurements and relatively objective, so lack of assessor knowledge is unlikely to bias measurement; moreover blinding was explicitly reported.

Quote: "Five (8%) patients in the enzyme replacement therapy group and four (14%) patients in the placebo group did not complete the study. Reasons for not completing the study in the treatment group included the following: hypersensitivity reactions related to the study medication (2), death unrelated to treatment (1), and switch to commercial enzyme replacement therapy (2). In the control group, the reasons included: adverse event not related to study medication (1), switch to commercial enzyme replacement therapy (1), and missed 78-week study appointments (2)." Also: "Only those patients with data points both at baseline and at 78 weeks were included in the efficacy analysis." and "Of patients with baseline ECG data, 89% had follow-up ECG data. Of patients with baseline echocardiographic data, 80% had follow up echocardiographic data." Comment: For the analysed 78-week cardiovascular outcomes, post-randomization missing outcome data were reported with per-arm numbers and reasons. Overall study non-completion was low to moderate and reasonably balanced between arms (8% vs 14%; 6 percentage-point difference), below thresholds that would usually indicate high risk. Although the efficacy analysis was completer-based, the reported loss does not show substantial or strongly differential attrition, and no cardiovascular-related discontinuations were reported.
